speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three with survivor Catheryne Morgan on August 15th, 1995, in New York, New York. I was asking you about your health. In-- just going back a little, in Italy, was it the shoulders that gave you problems as well there or what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The shoulders, and I also had the pleurisy. And that's what I was treated for in the Jew-- Jewish hospital in Trastevere in Rome for a long period of time. And then afterwards, it was very lovely. They sent me to a villa nearby Rome, which was what they call a sanatorium, which is to recover. And there I was recuperating there for probably many months. I have no recollection how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when it was that you gained your appetite back? Or you felt like--

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess gradually. But it was more or less in Italy. I just couldn't eat enough of that beautiful fruit, which I have seen the first time in many a years. But I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when it was that you got a zest for life back? When-- when you saw your Aunt Rose again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it started, I suppose, in Italy where I knew that things will get better from now on and when I realized that finally the war is over, and, of course, the sadness that-- I don't know whether I'd given up already hope then, I don't know, but I do know that I didn't think there is much hope that I find my parents ever, that they must have perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think it set into you that-- that your sister also wasn't alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I guess it never will. My [INAUDIBLE] I still feel that she's somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go back to Canada. How long did you live in Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a year and a half, and I stayed with the family Levine all this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, now, how old were you about now? Let's see--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, by now I am-- let's see. I was born in '28. 20-- well, I guess--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were almost 18.

speaker: interviewee
text: 18, 19 By then. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you speaking French now as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The English. Fortunately my father saw to it that we studied languages. And while my sister spoke a beautiful English, and, in fact, I remember she was getting book of the month club books in Czechoslovakia. My English, whatever I knew, was at least grammatically correct, and I managed. Then it has greatly improved in Montreal, and I spoke English only. Especially the family Levine. They didn't speak Italian or Czech, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard for you to meet other girls your age who hadn't had any of the similar life experiences? Was it hard to fit in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really don't know, because we were-- interestingly, on the ship, I made great friendship with two girls. One, another Catherine, who was also Czech. And one, Sylvia, who was Estonian and non-Jewish. And we became very close friends, and the three of us stuck together in Montreal. They-- we were all heading to Canada together, and we remained friends. And then through the Levines, I met some other young people. But I had no great difficulty adjusting to their way of life, so to speak. And I stayed with them up until one time. And then after I got paid, actually, for my work in the hospital, they got me a room with a very nice elderly couple near the hospital. So I sort of-- I didn't have a room of my own, but I stayed with them in one apartment and I left the Levines. And I was sort of, so to speak, on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that? How did you like living on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was very nice, because they were like-- like-- really like foster parents to me, this elderly couple. They had children of their own, but the children were grown. In fact, we visited them subsequently in Montreal fairly recently. And they were extremely nice and very protective. And I lived with them, and the hospital was walking distance where I worked. May I just say that in the interim, just-- I don't know how-- I found out about my uncle who indeed lived here, and I was corresponding with him. And by then I knew that I have an uncle. If-- if I ever come to America, I will be able to stay with my uncle and his daughter, Margaret.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you still want to come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Because in the meantime, I found out that Aunt Rose was not allowed to come to Canada, but for-- through the hire, she was brought to New York, in fact. So she was already here. And how on earth she managed to do that-- she was barely here a few months. She came to visit me in Canada, because I was not allowed to come, and she came, actually, to visit. So it was a reunion. And she told me that they are working on my papers then. It's just a matter of time that I will join her in New York also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the date that you did come to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in 19-- December 3rd. About-- actually it would be January. It was the night of New Year's night 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember? How did you get to New York? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. And I said, finally, I'm coming to New York. And it was at night. It was a night train. And at the first day of light, I said-- somebody said, the conductor, you are in America. And I always jokingly said the first thing I saw in America was Sing Sing. We'd just passed through Ossining, and I saw Sing Sing. But we got to New York, right to Grand Central Station. I have a recollection of my aunt, uncle, and their daughter were all waiting for me here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live with them at first? Your uncle? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My aunt was able to have a room, rooming with an elderly lady, and I went immediately. Stayed with her. But it was in the vicinity of where my uncle lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did you do when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first I didn't stay with my uncle. And being that I worked in the hospital, I had a very nice letter of recommendation for one of the big hospitals. And, indeed, I went for an interview. And as I was going, my uncle read and he found an ad that a private physician is looking for someone. And he doesn't want anybody trained, because he likes to train in the specialty that he practice himself. So I come back from the interview, and I said, look, they're going to let me know whether they're accepting me. He said, look, I want you to go. This sounds so good, and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my aunt and I set out from Brooklyn to come. We got lost, but we found 104 East 40th Street, Dr. Solomon. And we enter and there were, like, four or five other girls, and I wanted to immediately turn away. I said, look, I have no experience. I speak with an accent. I said, sure, sure, the hospital wants me.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She made me stay. And lo and behold, Dr. Solomon hired me. And I stayed with-- I can't keep a job-- I stayed with him some 20 odd years until he gave up the practice in-- in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was it like adjusting to New York? Did you like it? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved it. I commuted from Brooklyn. And, again, I was so fortunate. Dr. Solomon and his wife, they were extremely kind and understanding. And, again, I can only say they were like second parents and they treated me beautifully and were more understanding. They had a little daughter, Cathy, with whom I'm now-- since they passed away, she is like my own family. And things were very nice for me. I commuted, as I said, and I worked on my own. I had a lovely position. I started to work for him, and he taught me everything I knew. He was a urologist, and I needed to know about z-ray, and he told me. And then he said, look, it's OK with me, but I think you ought to go and have special schooling for it so you can be licensed. And he's the one who urged me. And indeed, I did. And I became a licensed x-ray technician and surgical assistant in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it hard making friends in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I seemed to have somehow found a group of young people. Some of them were also survivors, and I had contact with them. But I was barely in New York when I met Fred. And if I may, I must tell this story, because this is one for the books. In the meantime, my uncle came out. This doctor, as I said, was a kidney specialist, and my uncle had a serious kidney problem and the doctor had to operate on him, I'm happy to say, successfully. And his one and only daughter Margaret, my cousin, was so grateful that we took such good care of him, of course special care and all that, that after he got well-- she worked for a ring casting company-- she wanted to give me something. And since I didn't have a thing, she brought home a number of rings. I say, you pick. One was a little pearl, one was a green stone, one, whatever. You pick whatever you like. Well, I chose garnet, which is a Czech stone. And the reason why I'm saying that it's so important, had I not chosen this, I would have never met Fred, because after I saved up $15, I was anxious to get a pair of matching earrings. And somebody told me that a man from Czechoslovakia, who, indeed, I know-- knew his wife and him from Italy, they have an import business on garnets on 47th Street. So I went to look for him. And lo and behold, I meet Fred. He introduced us. Fred happened to be there. Had I chosen any other stone, I would have never gone to 47th Street, and I would have never met Fred.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this that you and Fred met?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 19-- 1951, end of the year. '51, '52 then. We went, we met.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you date, or do you remember your first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We dated till-- and we got married then in 19-- we were-- we got engaged on my birthday in 1954. And we got-- I mean '53. I'm sorry. And in January 3rd, 1954, we were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did Fred do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fred was always in the watch business. And he just happened to be in this place on 47th Street at the time when I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- where is Fred from originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fred is originally from Vienna. And while he went through some hardship himself trying to escape via France, and the France had thrown him into jail and eventually to Dachau, he did come out. And Fred's mother and father and sister Elaine, they all came out of Vienna in 1939 and lived here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was fortunate enough to meet his parents, and especially his mother, who will be always in my heart. A very special lady. On my wedding date, Fred later told me, called her aside, and told her-- told Fred to be extra special to me because Catheryne has no parents to go back to.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in Elaine and Max's home in Yonkers, New York. I never wanted a big wedding, since I didn't have family. And it was a beautiful wedding with a chuppah and a rabbi in Elaine's home. And Dr. and Mrs. Solomon were there and the little family, Elaine's daughter and son, and a few friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: After all that you had been through, did-- do you feel Jewish? Do you celebrate the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much. In fact, Fred always teases me because, while I don't come from a religious background-- incidentally, I just must say a very comical thing. My grandfather was a real bon vivant, and he went to a Carnival in Nice. And in those days, I mean, it was the biggest deal. I mean, how many people fly to Nice? But the interesting thing about it, his grandchildren, my sister, George, and me, as a gift he brought back bananas from Josephine Baker's skirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I'm trying to say is that while I didn't see it by example-- although Passover was kept in my grandmother's home-- although I didn't see it so much at home, I am religious to a sense that I never gave up my belief in God. I do light candles every Friday, and I do keep the holidays. And whenever I can, I go to a synagogue, especially during holidays, but we don't belong to a formal synagogue as such. And how long have you and Fred been married now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 40 Years. Since 1954. Happily married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I never had. No. Fred has a son from a previous marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever think, why you? Why did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as so many of us feel. And all I can say is that God must have had a special purpose indeed, because I was a scrawny little kid and grown men and soldiers couldn't survive, of course, the horrors and trials and tribulations. There must have been a reason. And I always felt, and I feel my philosophy of life has become to help others. Perhaps that's why I was in the medical field. And, in fact, I have now worked in New York and have just recently given it up. And now I do volunteer work at Sloan Kettering and Memorial Hospital, and it gives me immense satisfaction to make sure that I make at least one person each time feel better about her illness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to tell your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I'm really ever so grateful that I have this opportunity to do so, because I'm the only surviving member of my little family as well as my extended family. There is just no one else. And if not me, who? And, as the saying goes, and if not now, when? I mean, I'm not that old, but I'm not the youngest. In fact, when we go to many of the gatherings, it truly pains me to see that so many are not there anymore and many of them are, indeed, in an advanced age. So I was relatively young, and I'm still here and I'm glad that I can tell my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still friendly with any of the survivors that you had been through the war with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know any that I had been through, except there is one person. Her-- she's still here. She's alive. Her name is Ettu Goldstein, whose kindness I shall never forget. While we were in Auschwitz, she was obviously assigned to the kitchen, and I remember that one night she smuggled some food to my mother's sister, Rose, and me. And I'm still in touch with her. She lives in Florida. I call her religiously. And I, of course, will never, ever forget it. And it was probably she was risking her own very life to do so, but she managed to smuggle in food to us. Probably several times, I don't know. I remembered one definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never did go back. No. I don't know. I have mixed emotions about it. While in Czechoslovakia itself I don't have horrible memories, it was really in Hungary, somehow I just did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to any parts of Europe that you had been to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before? No. No. And I never went back to Germany. And I never did go back to Bergen-Belsen. I wanted to go for the big 50th anniversary, but I sort of gave that-- gave up the idea. In a way I wanted to be-- be there and-- and be happy in the fact that here we are, about a handful of us who can tell that indeed it did happen. On the other hand, I didn't. I went to Israel, to Jerusalem, and 1983 for the big reunion at that time. That was the only one that I participated in. But I didn't go back to Germany or to Poland, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what happened to Aunt Rose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aunt Rose after-- stayed here in New York. And, of course, we always lived very nearby. She worked herself in the health field, and then she wanted to go and-- her son, meantime, moved away from Rome. As a matter of fact, he lived in Hawaii, and she helped him to come here. And then she moved to Hawaii herself. But she wasn't too happy there. And she became, like, unfortunately the wandering Jew. She never wanted to have roots any place. And then she lived for one in Switzerland. And Fred treated her truly as though she was my own mother. Every year I would spend a month with her in Switzerland until in 1977 she became ill. And I went to Switzerland to see her and to see what I can do for her, and she said she wants to go to Israel. So unbeknown even to Fred, we picked ourselves up and I took her to Israel. She wanted to stay there. And I found a very nice place for her, and I left her there. Unfortunately, barely a few weeks I'm at home, and she just couldn't adjust. And she asked me to come back, which I did the second time. And she stayed on a little while. I came back again. Then in April I went to get her and brought her back to New York. She stayed with us a little while. Unfortunately we don't have an extra room for her. We tried to get her something, but by then her health seemed to fail. She had a heart condition, and we thought that the best thing would be to put her somewhere where she could get some care. And there was this beautiful brand new nursing home in Queens, and I was able to get her a lovely room, and she was quite happy there. But she didn't stay long. She became ill, we took her to the hospital, and she never returned. She died of heart failure August 27, 1977.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the story about the necklace you're waring.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very special piece of jewelry. As a young girl in Italy, of course, I had nothing. And as every young girl wants some jewelry, but they couldn't afford. We had these $3 among us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, when we lived in Ostia Lida di Roma, my aunt managed to buy this on the beach of Ostia for $0.25 American. And it became my absolute one and only and most favorite piece. It was restrung many, many times. But needless to say, I would not trade it with a necklace of diamonds. Have there been any decisions you've made or things you've done that were influences by your Shoah experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think everything I do, in a way. My outlook on life, I don't know. Some friends say that I'm an envy of all of them because I find joy in simple things of life. I mean, I don't want to sound like Pollyanna, but material things truly are not my priority. When I see sunshine, as you can see, I'm wearing yellow. They are always-- my friends think I'm synonymous with sunshine. I rejoice in it and flowers and every little bit of life. And I try to be good and do kindness whenever I can. And as my friends will attest to it, I'm always the first to come to day aid whenever they need me, and so does my family. And I try to be good. That's my primary religion, to try to be good and decent. And no matter what your religion, I see no distinction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's been almost 50 years since the war ended, do you have any special message or lesson for the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message would be summed up-- it's interesting you should ask. We were in Vienna just a few weeks ago, and there is a memorial in the middle of Vienna right next to their opera, because-- Fred, of course, he saw it himself-- they took elderly Jews, and on all four, they had to scrub the street with a toothbrush. And there is, indeed, a statue depicting such a Jew. And as-- whenever I'm in Vienna I make sure that I go there and I place some flowers. And this trip, as all others, I've been there. And a group of schoolchildren happened to come with a teacher, and I asked whether they know what it is. They'd never been there, and the teacher was obviously taking them for a reason. And in my best German, which is quite atrocious, but I managed to explain what cruelty to men does and to learn to be good and to respect each other whatever your religion. Whether you are Muslim or Catholic or whatever it is, just be kind and good, for we all belong to the same race. Well, it was most gratifying. The children all loved it, and the teacher gave me round of applause. And the teacher came over to me and thanked me so much. She said, that's the best lesson they could, indeed, have from somebody who has firsthand experience of hatred that is caused by hating each other and each other's religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that I didn't ask you during this interview or any little story you might want to tell from any part of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really can't think of anything in particular. There are many little incidents, but, you know, you can't think of them all. But primarily, as I said, I became a well-adjusted person. I still suffer of my chronic dislocation, but I live with it and I don't let it bother me. I do the best I can. I can't do any physical labor, but, as I said, I managed, in my profession, to work all these many years, which was most satisfying. And I love my family. Fred's sister-- we have a very small family-- Fred's sister Elaine, her son David, her daughter Ingrid, their son Michael, and we are very close. And I love my friends. And they all say I'm very giving, and I just love to see when people enjoy coming here and I can cause any joy in any form or shape to anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do your niece and nephews, do they know your story? Or your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: To some extent. He's-- the niece-- well, the-- oh, the niece does, yes. Her son is too young, but her niece done. As the years went by, for some reason she always was told not to ask questions. And you see, at the beginning, truly, we were always told, especially Aunt Rose. I was young. I adjusted. But Aunt Rose talked about it always. And we would hear reprimands. Oh, you have to forget. It's gone with you. Your new life is starting. She would always bring out different details, but it was suppressed and people really didn't want to hear. And I never volunteered to talk to anyone about it. They already knew of, course, that I'm a survivor. And I never hid it. And so we spoke about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still have dreams or any nightmares about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never had nightmares, so to speak. No. But it's with me. There isn't, as I said, a day or a moment. And to this day I can't look at a chimney. We had to move from somewhere because there was a chimney. And things like that, of course, that just automatically-- the human mind plays funny tricks on you. They remind you of things even when you don't want to. And, of course, my day starts with a prayer for my martyred family. There's just one incident, if I may say, which will always be in my heart. Aunt Rose took it upon herself after the war to go to Auschwitz where she took some earth, since we don't have a grave at all for my family. And she took it to Budapest where her father, who happened to be by chance at the time when he collapsed and died of a heart attack, is buried. And she made a memorial on the grave. And it's became my [INAUDIBLE]. And Fred and I see to it that it's being taken care of. And whenever we are in Europe, we go there personally to see that, indeed, it's in order, since that's the only remaining monument to our martyred family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many languages do you speak today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's see. My English, hopefully it's of any-- any good. I speak German. I manage in German. I speak Hungarian. I speak Czech and Slovak. I understand Polish and Russian. My Italian is very good. And I speak quite a bit of Hebrew. I was just practicing it this morning as well. I went here to [INAUDIBLE], because I wanted to learn from scratch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you and your husband, when you were first married, did you speak a certain language at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Always English only. My German was atrocious. And I must say in all honesty, I don't make much effort to learn better German. While I really am-- I must say that at first, while every time I lived in Europe and I saw somebody at the age where I knew they were soldiers-- I saw an SS uniform on them. I'm able to distinguish now and I don't make a blanket cover. I'm sure there are some good ones-- excuse me-- and in fact, we made friends with a lovely family from Germany, the [? Irvings, ?] who, of course, knew about my past. And I never made it a secret that I'm Jewish and so is Fred. And they were ever so nice that one day they drove us to Dachau because I said to Fred, but for the grace of God, here you come with me to look at what you left in '39. And we correspond and I love them dearly. I truly don't feel that I should be bitter for all that happened to every German.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop now and we'll do your photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I want to just thank you for doing this interview with us today. I think it's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can thank you. And I say again how very grateful I am that I do have this opportunity to tell my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
